Apple___Apple_scab.description: A serious fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis that affects apple leaves, buds, and fruits.
symptoms: Olive-green to brown spots on leaves
prevention: Remove and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
Apple___Apple_scab.usage_instructions: Apply fungicides in early spring when green tips appear and repeat every 7-10 days during rainy periods.
Apple___Apple_scab.recovery_tips: Increase potassium and phosphorus to boost plant immunity.
Apple___Apple_scab.crop_safety: Avoid using sulfur on sulfur-sensitive varieties like Cox's Orange Pippin.
Apple___Apple_scab.expert_suggestion: Focus on sanitation. Removing old leaves significantly reduces the spore count for next season.

Apple___Black_rot.description: A fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa that affects fruits, leaves, and bark.
symptoms: Small purple spots on leaves expanding into 'frog-eye' lesions
Sunken brown spots on fruit with dark concentric rings
causes: Fungus overwintering in cankers or mummified fruit
prevention: Prune out dead wood and cankers
Remove mummified fruit from the tree
Maintain tree vigor through proper watering
Apple___Black_rot.usage_instructions: Apply copper-based sprays during the dormant season and early bloom stage.
Apple___Black_rot.recovery_tips: Severe pruning of infected limbs is necessary for long-term recovery.
Apple___Black_rot.crop_safety: Copper can be phytotoxic to some varieties if used in high heat.
Apple___Black_rot.expert_suggestion: Sterilize pruning tools with 10% bleach solution between cuts to prevent spread.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: A complex fungal disease that requires both apple trees and Junipers (cedars) to complete its life cycle.
symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces
Small tube-like structures on the underside of leaves
causes: Spores carried by wind in humid conditions
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.usage_instructions: Spray from pink bud stage through petal fall every 7-10 days.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.recovery_tips: Ensure the tree is well-hydrated to withstand the stress of defoliation.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.crop_safety: Generally safe for all apple varieties if used as directed.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.expert_suggestion: If you see orange 'galls' on nearby junipers, treat your apple trees immediately.

Apple___healthy.description: The leaf appears healthy and free from noticeable fungal or bacterial pathogens.
Apple___healthy.usage_instructions: Feed in early spring before new growth starts.
Apple___healthy.expert_suggestion: Healthy leaves are a sign of good soil health. Keep the soil pH between 6.0 and 7.0 for optimal nutrient uptake.

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant shows no signs of disease or nutrient deficiency.
Blueberry___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply fertilizer in early spring and again in late spring.
Blueberry___healthy.recovery_tips: Maintain current care. Monitor for birds during fruiting.
Blueberry___healthy.expert_suggestion: Blueberries love acidic soil. If leaves turn yellow with green veins, check soil pH immediately.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: A fungal disease caused by Podosphaera clandestina, appearing as white powdery growth on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: White, flour-like coating on young leaves
causes: Dry soil combined with humid canopy
prevention: Water from below to avoid wetting foliage
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.usage_instructions: Apply sulfur at the first sign of mildew. Do not apply when temperatures exceed 90°F.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.recovery_tips: Remove heavily infected shoots to stop the spread.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.crop_safety: Sulfur can damage foliage if applied in high heat.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.expert_suggestion: Applying a diluted milk spray (1:9 ratio) can sometimes control mild infections organically.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry plant is healthy and showing vigorous growth.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.expert_suggestion: Good drainage is key for cherries. They hate 'wet feet' which can lead to root rot.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: One of the most destructive diseases of corn, caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis.
symptoms: Small tan spots with yellow halos
Long, rectangular lesions parallel to leaf veins
causes: High humidity and long periods of leaf wetness
prevention: Rotate crops away from corn for at least one year
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.usage_instructions: Apply fungicides when symptoms appear on the third leaf below the ear or higher.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.recovery_tips: Early detection is critical. Once it covers 50% of the leaf, yield loss is significant.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.crop_safety: Apply according to label rates to avoid resistance development.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.expert_suggestion: The rectangular shape of the spots is a key identifier. It's limited by the leaf veins.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: A fungal disease caused by Puccinia sorghi that appears as cinnamon-brown pustules on corn leaves.
symptoms: Pustules appear on BOTH upper and lower leaf surfaces
causes: Spores blown in from southern regions
prevention: Plant early to avoid late-season spore peaks
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.usage_instructions: Fungicides are usually only economical for high-value seed corn or sweet corn.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.recovery_tips: Maintain nitrogen levels to help the plant outgrow the infection.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.crop_safety: Safe when used at recommended growth stages.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.expert_suggestion: Unlike Southern Rust, Common Rust pustules are found on both sides of the leaf. Use this for identification.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: A fungal disease caused by Exserohilum turcicum, known for large cigar-shaped lesions.
symptoms: Lesions may have dark spores in the center
Heavily infected leaves may gray and die
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.usage_instructions: Apply fungicides at tasseling stage (VT) if symptoms are present.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.recovery_tips: Minimize moisture on leaves if using irrigation.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.expert_suggestion: The 'cigar-shape' is the hallmark of this disease. It can spread very rapidly in wet summers.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn plant is healthy and producing strong foliage.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.usage_instructions: Side-dress nitrogen when corn is at V4-V6 stage.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.expert_suggestion: Monitor for 'firing' or yellowing at the bottom of the plant, which may indicate nitrogen deficiency even if no disease is present.

Grape___Black_rot.description: One of the most damaging grape diseases, caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwellii.
symptoms: Small tan spots on leaves with dark borders
Tiny black dots (fruiting bodies) within leaf spots
Grapes turn into dry, black, shriveled 'mummies'
causes: Warm, wet weather from bloom through fruit set
prevention: Remove all mummified fruit during winter pruning
Grape___Black_rot.usage_instructions: Apply preventative sprays from early shoot growth until 2-4 weeks after bloom.
Grape___Black_rot.recovery_tips: Prune out infected clusters immediately to prevent spread to healthy fruit.
Grape___Black_rot.crop_safety: Do not use Mancozeb within 66 days of harvest.
Grape___Black_rot.expert_suggestion: Early season control is the only way to save the fruit. Once the grapes start rotting, it's too late for that cluster.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: A complex fungal disease affecting the wood and foliage of grapevines, often leading to sudden vine decline.
symptoms: Yellow or red 'tiger-stripe' patterns on leaves
Tiny dark spots on fruit skins (measles)
causes: Pathogenic fungi entering through pruning wounds
fertilizers: Soil amendments to reduce vine stress
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).usage_instructions: Focus on preventative pruning care. No effective chemical cure once in the wood.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).recovery_tips: In severe cases, the vine may need to be retrained from a healthy sucker.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).expert_suggestion: The 'tiger-stripe' leaf pattern is very distinct. It often affects only one branch initially.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: A late-season fungal disease caused by Pseudocercospora vitis.
symptoms: Dark brown, irregular spots on leaves
Spots may coalesce into large blighted areas
Premature leaf drop in late summer
causes: Poor air circulation within the canopy
prevention: Leaf pulling to increase airflow around clusters
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.usage_instructions: Apply copper sprays if symptoms appear early in the season. Late infections usually don't need treatment.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.recovery_tips: Healthy vines will recover the following year.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.crop_safety: Copper can be harsh on young leaves; use with caution.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.expert_suggestion: This is usually a late-season disease and may not significantly impact fruit quality unless defoliation is severe.

Grape___healthy.description: The grapevine is healthy and producing lush foliage.
fertilizers: Magnesium sulfate (Epsom salts) if soil is deficient
Grape___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply fertilizer in early spring as buds break.
Grape___healthy.recovery_tips: Healthy. Keep up with vineyard hygiene.
Grape___healthy.expert_suggestion: Healthy grapes need lots of sun. Ensure no over-shading from nearby trees.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: The most devastating citrus disease worldwide, caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter and spread by the Asian Citrus Psyllid.
fertilizers: Enhanced nutritional programs (Zinc, Manganese, Iron sprays)
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).usage_instructions: Focus on foliar nutrition to keep the tree productive as long as possible.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).recovery_tips: Currently no cure. Management focuses on slowing spread and maintaining yield.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).crop_safety: Follow pesticide safety guidelines for citrus.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).expert_suggestion: The yellowing is 'asymmetrical' - if you fold the leaf in half, the yellow pattern won't match. This distinguishes it from nutrient deficiency.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni affecting leaves, fruit, and twigs.
symptoms: Small water-soaked spots that turn purple/black
Deep pits or cracks on fruit
prevention: Avoid excess nitrogen which promotes soft growth
Peach___Bacterial_spot.usage_instructions: Apply copper in late dormancy. Use Oxytetracycline during the growing season.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.recovery_tips: Prune out infected twigs during winter.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.crop_safety: Copper can cause leaf damage ('phytotoxicity') if used after bloom.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.expert_suggestion: The 'shot-hole' appearance is very characteristic. Don't confuse it with insect damage.

Peach___healthy.description: The peach tree is healthy and growing well.
prevention: Thin fruit to prevent branch breakage
Peach___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply fertilizer in a 2-3 foot band around the drip line.
Peach___healthy.expert_suggestion: Peach trees are short-lived. Keep them healthy through heavy annual pruning to stimulate new fruiting wood.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: A common and destructive disease of peppers in warm, humid climates, caused by Xanthomonas campestris.
symptoms: Small, irregular chocolate-brown spots on leaves
Spots may have a yellow halo
Leaves may turn yellow and drop
prevention: Rotate crops (no peppers or tomatoes for 2-3 years)
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.usage_instructions: Spray every 7-14 days during wet, warm weather.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.recovery_tips: Remove heavily infected plants to stop the spread.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.crop_safety: Copper is safe for peppers but can accumulate in soil over years.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.expert_suggestion: Avoid working in the garden when plants are wet, as the bacteria spread easily on hands and tools.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The pepper plant is healthy and producing well.
prevention: Stake plants to support heavy fruit
Pepper,_bell___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply phosphorus when first flowers appear.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.expert_suggestion: Peppers need lots of calcium. If the bottom of the fruit turns black, it's a calcium deficiency, not a disease.

Potato___Early_blight.description: A fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani, characterized by 'target-like' leaf spots.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern)
Stem lesions and tuber rot possible
causes: Warm/wet conditions followed by dry periods
prevention: Irrigate early in the day so leaves dry quickly
Potato___Early_blight.usage_instructions: Apply fungicides when the first symptoms appear on lower leaves.
Potato___Early_blight.recovery_tips: Healthy plants can 'outgrow' mild infections if fertilized properly.
Potato___Early_blight.crop_safety: Safe when used according to label.
Potato___Early_blight.expert_suggestion: The 'target' pattern in the spots is the most reliable way to identify Early Blight.

Potato___Late_blight.description: The infamous disease that caused the Irish Potato Famine, caused by the water mold Phytophthora infestans. Extremely aggressive.
symptoms: White fuzzy growth on the underside of edges
causes: Infected seed potatoes or cull piles
Potato___Late_blight.usage_instructions: Fungicides MUST be used preventatively when the 'Late Blight Forecast' is high.
Potato___Late_blight.recovery_tips: If late blight is found in the garden, pull and destroy plants immediately. Do NOT compost.
Potato___Late_blight.crop_safety: Safe for plants, but check pre-harvest interval.
Potato___Late_blight.expert_suggestion: This disease can kill a whole field in days. The white fuzzy growth on the underside is the sign of active spore production.

Potato___healthy.description: The potato plant is growing vigorously and showing no signs of blight.
prevention: Hill plants to protect tubers from light/blight spores
Potato___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply potassium at planting and at bloom stage.
Potato___healthy.recovery_tips: Healthy. Keep hilling up the soil.
Potato___healthy.expert_suggestion: Keep the leaves dry. Overhead irrigation is the number one friend of potato diseases.

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry plant is healthy and preparing for fruit production.
prevention: Prune out spent floricanes after harvest
Raspberry___healthy.usage_instructions: Fertilize once in early spring and once in mid-summer.
Raspberry___healthy.recovery_tips: Healthy. Keep mulch around the base.
Raspberry___healthy.expert_suggestion: Raspberries have shallow roots; don't cultivate the soil too deeply around them.

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean plant is healthy and fixing nitrogen effectively.
Soybean___healthy.usage_instructions: Potassium is best applied before or at planting.
Soybean___healthy.recovery_tips: Healthy. Monitor for Japanese beetles during flowering.
Soybean___healthy.expert_suggestion: Check for nitrogen-fixing nodules on the roots. They should be pink inside if they are active.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: A very common fungal disease of cucurbits that looks like flour dusted on leaves.
symptoms: Spots spread to cover entire leaf
Leaves eventually turn yellow and brittle
Squash___Powdery_mildew.usage_instructions: Treat at first sign. Repeat every 7 days as long as conditions are humid.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.recovery_tips: Severely infected leaves can be removed to reduce spore load.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.crop_safety: Sulfur can burn squash leaves if temperatures are above 85°F.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.expert_suggestion: A solution of 1 teaspoon baking soda and 1 quart water can be a good home remedy for early stage mildew.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Fungal disease caused by Diplocarpon earlianum that affects the foliage of strawberry plants.
symptoms: Small purplish spots on the upper leaf surface
Spots expand and the centers remain purple (unlike Leaf Spot)
Blighted scorched appearance on leaf edges
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.usage_instructions: Apply fungicides in early spring as new growth starts.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.recovery_tips: Healthy plants can tolerate some leaf scorch without yield loss.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.crop_safety: Follow pre-harvest intervals for all fungicides.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.expert_suggestion: Drip irrigation is much better than sprinklers for Strawberries to prevent this disease.

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry plant is healthy and producing runners or fruit.
prevention: Remove dead leaves in late winter
Strawberry___healthy.usage_instructions: Avoid nitrogen in spring as it leads to soft fruit; fertilize after harvest.
Strawberry___healthy.expert_suggestion: Keeping berries off the soil with straw mulch is the best way to prevent fruit rot diseases.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: A serious bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas species affecting all above-ground parts of the tomato plant.
symptoms: Small, greasy black spots on leaves
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.usage_instructions: Apply copper strictly every 7 days during wet weather.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.recovery_tips: Prune out infected lower leaves and destroy them.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.crop_safety: Copper is safe for tomatoes but limit use in extreme heat.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.expert_suggestion: This is often confused with early blight, but the 'greasy' appearance of the spots is a key indicator for bacteria.

Tomato___Early_blight.description: A fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani that usually starts on the lower leaves of tomato plants.
symptoms: Lower leaves yellow and die first
prevention: Mulch under plants to prevent soil splash
fertilizers: Potassium to boost cell wall strength
Tomato___Early_blight.usage_instructions: Spray every 7-10 days once disease is spotted or as a preventative.
Tomato___Early_blight.recovery_tips: The plant can still produce well if only the lower leaves are affected.
Tomato___Early_blight.expert_suggestion: Trimming the bottom-most leaves is your best defense. It prevents soil-borne spores from splashing up.

Tomato___Late_blight.description: A highly destructive disease caused by Phytophthora infestans that can kill plants overnight.
symptoms: White mold on underside of patches
causes: Infected transplants or nearby potato fields
Tomato___Late_blight.usage_instructions: Requires regular preventative spraying if conditions are 'Late Blight positive'.
Tomato___Late_blight.recovery_tips: Recovery is unlikely. Focus on protecting uninfected plants.
Tomato___Late_blight.crop_safety: Late blight spores are extremely mobile; notify neighbors if found.
Tomato___Late_blight.expert_suggestion: This is the 'Terminator' of tomatoes. If you see white fuzz under dark leaves in rainy weather, act immediately!

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: A fungal disease caused by Passalora fulva, most common in greenhouses but can occur in the garden during humid spells.
symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to gray velvety growth on the undersides
Leaves eventually turn brown and wither
causes: Poor air circulation within the plant canopy
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.usage_instructions: Spray fungicides if symptoms appear and humidity remains high.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.recovery_tips: Reducing humidity is more effective than any fungicide.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.expert_suggestion: The 'velvety' growth on the underside is the smoking gun for Leaf Mold.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: A common fungal disease (Septoria lycopersici) that creates small, numerous spots on lower leaves.
symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark borders and gray centers
Tiny black specks in the center of spots
Leaves turn yellow and fall off
prevention: Wait 3 years between planting tomatoes in same spot
fertilizers: Fish emulsion to help leaf regrowth
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.usage_instructions: Fungicide applications are often necessary if the disease appears during the first fruit set.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.recovery_tips: Septoria does not infect the fruit, so you can still harvest healthy tomatoes.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.expert_suggestion: Septoria spots are much smaller/more circular than Early Blight spots and have distinct grey centers.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Tiny arachnids that suck the life out of tomato leaves, especially during hot, dry weather.
symptoms: Fine yellow or white stippling (dots) on leaves
Thin silken webs on the undersides of leaves
Leaves turn bronze and dry out
causes: Over-use of broad-spectrum insecticides that kill their predators
prevention: Mist the plants with water occasionally
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.usage_instructions: Spray Neem Oil late in the day, ensuring you coat the UNDERSIDES of the leaves.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.recovery_tips: A strong blast of water can physically remove mites and their webs.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.crop_safety: Neem oil is safe but avoid spraying in direct sun.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.expert_suggestion: Spider mites are tiny. Shake a leaf over white paper; if the 'dust' starts crawling, you have mites.

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: A fungal disease caused by Corynespora cassiicola, appearing as large, target-like lesions on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Large, brown leaf spots with distinct rings
Spots on fruit that look like sunken craters
Defoliation starting from the middle of the plant
Tomato___Target_Spot.usage_instructions: Fungicides are most effective when applied at the first sign of lesions.
Tomato___Target_Spot.recovery_tips: Severe cases may require removal of the entire plant to protect others.
Tomato___Target_Spot.expert_suggestion: Target Spot fruit lesions are deeper and more sunken than Early Blight fruit lesions.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: A devastating viral disease spread by the Silverleaf Whitefly.
symptoms: Leaves turn yellow at the margins
Flowers drop, and fruit production stops
prevention: Use yellow sticky traps to monitor whiteflies
Cover plants with fine mesh row covers
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.usage_instructions: Controlling the whitefly is the ONLY way to manage this virus.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.recovery_tips: There is no cure for the virus. Infected plants should be bagged and removed.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.crop_safety: Handle infected plants with care to avoid spread via hands.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.expert_suggestion: The upward 'cupping' of small leaves is very characteristic. If the plant looks like a 'bonsai', it's likely TYLCV.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: A very hardy virus that can persist on tools and in the soil for years.
symptoms: Mottled green and yellow patterns (mosaic) on leaves
prevention: Wash hands with soap before touching plants
Don't smoke or use tobacco near tomato plants
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.usage_instructions: Focus on hygiene. Avoid touching plants unnecessarily.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.recovery_tips: The plant will not recover. It can stay alive but will produce poor fruit and spread virus.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.crop_safety: Extremely contagious. Sterilize all tools used on infected plants.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.expert_suggestion: Tobacco users should be especially careful, as the virus can be on cigarettes/tobacco products.

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato plant is healthy and thriving.
prevention: Continue deep watering at the base
fertilizers: Epsom salts (1 tbsp per plant for magnesium)
Tomato___healthy.usage_instructions: Apply fertilizer every 4 weeks after the first fruit set.
Tomato___healthy.expert_suggestion: Healthy tomatoes need consistent moisture to prevent blossom end rot, which is a physiological disorder, not a disease.
